text: fat is 3.3g. saturated fat, cholesterol, and omega3 are doing most of the work. for dairy, the fat side can either add richness or drag the whole profile down.
carbs is 4.8g. sugar, glycemic index, and starch are doing most of the work. for dairy, the question is whether the payoff is worth the baggage.
protein is 3.2g. protein quality is clearly one of the selling points. for dairy, useful protein can rescue a lot of weaker traits.
vitamin b12 at 19% DV, vitamin a at 5% DV stand out most. that is not enough to move the verdict very far on its own.
calcium at 9% DV, zinc at 4% DV, magnesium at 2% DV stand out most. that stays fairly limited overall.

pros first. easy protein delivery for normal diets because dairy gets real credit for convenience. clear calcium identity because that is a core category strength. slots into meals very easily because practical versatility matters a lot for dairy.

the drawbacks next. not everyone tolerates dairy well because that is a real use-case limitation. fat quality varies wildly across dairy because some dairy is clean, some really is not. processing changes the category a lot because the label alone does not tell the whole story.
Whole Milk is A tier. Best when the protein payoff or fermentation angle outweighs the fat and sodium tradeoffs.
